title: Mist Bell Test
desc: When the fog closes, the garrison has to listen its way across Cordac Falls.

steps.text:
Light works well at Cordac Falls until it doesn't. When the fog thickens enough to swallow the rails and bridges whole, the garrison switches to sound: bronze bells on the upper posts, speaking tubes in the sheltered cuttings, fixed call patterns that carry through mist better than shouting ever could. Today's test is routine, which means it matters. The day the bells fail should not be the day anyone learns it.

The signal sergeant lays out the chart of tones on a crate lid. "Upper bells if you want wind and cold," she says. "Lower tube station if you want condensate and echo. Either test will tell us what still works when sight gives up."

options.label: Test the upper bell line from post to post
Clear and test the lower speaking tubes

steps.text:
The upper bell posts run along the exposed edge where the wind comes hardest off the gorge. Each bell is tuned to a slightly different pitch so a warning can be read by sequence instead of volume. You strike the first, wait for the answer from the next post, record the delay, then move on. Most ring true. One sounds muddy, its note flattened by a damp leather clapper wrap that has swollen in the spray.

You replace the wrap, sound the bell again, and this time the note cuts through the mist clean and bright. The answering tone from the next post comes back half a breath sooner, clear enough to raise the hair on your arms despite the cold. The signal sergeant studies your marks when you return and seems satisfied by the neatness of the sequence. "Good. If the gorge decides to blind us today, at least it will still have to hear us."

options.label: Hang the chart back in its case and leave the bells ready

steps.text:
The lower tube station is carved into the cliff behind a baffle wall where the wind cannot snatch every word on its way past. Bronze speaking tubes run from here to three nearby posts, but condensation loves them nearly as much as voices do. You uncap each throat, swab the interior dry, and clear a small clot of mineral grit from the second line before the signaler runs the call pattern.

The first message comes through thin but readable. The second, after the blockage is cleared, arrives so sharply it feels like someone speaking over your shoulder instead of from two cuttings away. That is the standard the sergeant wants. Not merely sound, but certainty. When the last test is logged, she folds the sheet and tucks it under one arm. "There. Fog can keep its tricks. We still have our own."

options.label: Cap the tubes and leave the station dry
